default_deck.name: Portuguese 100 Words
default_deck.description: Most common Portuguese words and their English translations

beginner.title: Basic Articles
beginner.content:
Portuguese definite articles (o/a/os/as) correspond to 'the' in English.
Indefinite articles (um/uma/uns/umas) correspond to 'a' or 'an'.
For example: 'o livro' (the book), 'uma casa' (a house).

beginner.title: Gender in Portuguese
beginner.content:
Most Portuguese nouns are either masculine or feminine.
Masculine nouns often end in '-o' and feminine nouns in '-a',
though there are exceptions.  Adjectives must agree in gender and number
with the nouns they modify.

beginner.title: Simple Prepositions
beginner.content:
Common prepositions include 'em' (in/at), 'de' (of/from), 'para' (for/to).
When combined with articles they contract: 'em' + 'a' = 'na', 'de' + 'o' = 'do'.

intermediate.title: Past Tenses
intermediate.content:
Portuguese has two main past tenses: the pretérito perfeito (simple past)
and the pretérito imperfeito (imperfect).  The simple past describes completed
actions, while the imperfect describes ongoing or habitual past actions.

intermediate.title: Using 'a gente'
intermediate.content:
In Brazilian Portuguese, 'a gente' is an informal way to say 'we'.
It always takes third person singular verb forms.  For example:
'A gente vai ao cinema' (We're going to the cinema).

intermediate.title: Direct and Indirect Object Pronouns
intermediate.content:
Direct object pronouns (me, te, o, a, nos, vos, os, as) replace the object of a verb.
Indirect object pronouns (me, te, lhe, nos, vos, lhes) indicate to whom or for whom.

advanced.title: Subjunctive Mood
advanced.content:
The subjunctive mood expresses doubt, desire, or uncertainty.
It has present, past and future forms and is often triggered by phrases like
'para que', 'a fim de que', or verbs expressing desire.

advanced.title: Conditional Sentences
advanced.content:
Conditional sentences in Portuguese use the conjunction 'se' (if).
They combine the imperfect subjunctive with the conditional or future tense
to express hypothetical situations.  For example: 'Se eu tivesse dinheiro, viajaria'.

advanced.title: Progressive Aspect and Gerund
advanced.content:
The gerund (‑ndo) conveys ongoing action.  Combine with 'estar' to form the
progressive: 'Estou estudando' (I am studying).
